Radio Times: Saturday Evening Lineup – 7th December 1979

Following the hustle and bustle of today's 'Grandstand' with David Coleman and the team, featuring the day's sporting highlights from football to figure skating, stay tuned for a splendid evening of entertainment on BBC One.

4:30 pm – 'Barnet Fair'
Gather the children and join us at the colourful 'Barnet Fair', where whimsy meets wonder in this delightful amalgam of music, story, and pantomime. This week, with the holiday season upon us, our merry troupe of performers presents "The Toymaker's Winterscape," an enchanting tale of mirth and melody to capture the imaginations of all our middle school audience. This half-hour of jovial escapades is sure to leave your young ones spellbound and singing.

5:00 pm – 'Basil Brush Show'
Boom! Boom! It's time for a burst of laughter with our favourite fox, Basil Brush, and Mr. Derek Fowlds. This week's episode sees Basil tangled up in a Yuletide caper that promises chuckles and mischief. So, gather round for this family show; at 30 minutes, it's just the right tickle for the funny bone before tea.

5:30 pm – 'The Traveller'
Adventure awaits with the enigmatic 'Traveller' in an all-new episode of the captivating sci-fantasy serial. In tonight's installment, "Echoes of Eternity," the Traveller and his companion, Sophie, warp to a distant star where ancient prophecies whisper and danger lurks behind every shadow. Can they unravel the mysteries of the Chronos Cube in time? This forty-five-minute journey through time and space is one that older children and adults alike won't want to miss.

6:15 pm – 'The News and Weather'
Stay informed with the latest headlines from around the globe followed by an in-depth weather forecast. Find out what's happening in the world and how to bundle up for the early December chill.

6:30 pm – 'The Generation Game'
It's Saturday night, and 'The Generation Game' is back on your screens, hosted by the ever-charismatic Bruce Forsyth. Watch as family teams clash in a series of uproarious games and crafty challenges to win the coveted conveyor belt of prizes. Laughter, suspense, and a touch of healthy family rivalry – it's all here in this hour-long spectacle of fun.

Tune in to BBC One for an evening of magical stories, laughter, intrigue, and family-friendly fun sure to brighten up your winter weekend. And don't turn that dial, as tonight's lineup will be followed by the delightful musical stylings of 'The Val Doonican Music Show' at 7:00 pm, adding a touch of melodic warmth to your cold December night.

So cozy up, pour a cup of tea, and let us entertain you. BBC One is the place to be this Saturday evening!

Radio 2, known for its more light-hearted and entertainment-focused content compared to the more speech and drama-oriented Radio 4, would be a fitting platform for a modern rendition of the show.

Considering the heritage and popularity of "The Navy Lark," it could be placed as a flagship programme in a weekend slot when audiences are more available to tune in for comedy and nostalgia, or perhaps in an early evening weekday slot when listeners might appreciate some humour to uplift their mood after work.

Here’s an example of where “The Navy Lark” revival could fit into the Radio 2 schedule:

Episode Title: "The Catch of the Day"

Episode Teaser:
Join the crew of HMS Troutbridge as they navigate the choppy waters of diplomacy and red tape in the dragnet of European fishing quotas. When the hapless crew finds themselves embroiled in a "fishing fiasco" out at sea, it's every sailor, officer, and nation for themselves!

Comedic Setup and Satirical Reveal:
After a bureaucratic mix-up, HMS Troutbridge is tasked with enforcing fishing quotas in the North Sea - a job they are woefully unprepared for. Captain Povey, attempting to show initiative, interprets the vague orders as a call to monitor fish stocks personally. Naturally, chaos ensues when the crew attempts to man a fishing operation under the guise of naval authority.

CPO Pertwee sees a business opportunity, attempting to sell the "accidentally" caught fish back to the local fishermen, while Wren Underwood passionately advocates for marine conservation, taping pamphlets on sustainable fishing to the hull of the ship.

The satirical edge comes to a head when the crew inadvertently incites an international incident. They challenge a vessel they suspect of illegal fishing, only to discover it's a diplomatic yacht hosting peace talks between EU nations on fishing rights – with a flustered new officer (played by Nigel Havers) left to smooth the diplomatic ripples, only to find that the opposite numbers are old adversaries from his days at the Naval College.

Aboard the diplomatic yacht, a grand dinner and negotiations over caviar and fine wine smash up against the Troutbridge's motley crew's comedic mismanagement and Pertwee's black-market fish pies. Ideals clash, nets are cut, and the only thing clear about the quotas is the vast sea of confusion they spawn - leading to a reveal that perhaps the biggest fish to fry isn't out in the ocean, but swimming in the sea of bureaucratic paperwork.

Havers is I think more inteligent than the Leslie Philips role,  but he's a more 'dashing' type of modern naval officer for the era..   Can you give him a sutiable comedic exchange with WREN Underwood?

Certainly, a comedic exchange between a dashing, modern Royal Navy officer played by Nigel Havers and WREN Underwood could go something like this:

---

**Setting**: The deck of HMS Troutbridge, where WREN Underwood is updating the ship's log with the latest (and highly confusing) fishing quota regulations.

**New Officer (played by Nigel Havers)**: "Ah, Wren Underwood, diligently at your post as always. I must say, your dedication to paperwork is as unwavering as the North Star."

**WREN Underwood**: "Paperwork might be the one thing aboard this vessel that doesn't sway, sir. These new quotas are more tangled than a fisherman's net!"

**New Officer**: "I couldn't agree more. It's as if these regulations are designed for the Kraken instead of the common sprat. Tell me, is there a clause for the event of accidentally catching a sea serpent—or perhaps a mermaid?"

**WREN Underwood**: "At this point, sir, I wouldn't be surprised if there was a special provision for part-time underwater basket weaving."

*(Both share a courteous chuckle, when suddenly a crash is heard from below deck.)*

**New Officer**: "What was that?"

Episode Teaser:
Arcades invade HMS Troutbridge in this week’s deviously digital escapade, "The Accidental Arcade!" Comedic Setup and Satirical Reveal:
As Captain Povey declaims the younger generation's obsession with blinking screens and bleeping noises, he's astonished to discover what appears to be a coin-operated arcade machine in the mess, complete with joystick, buttons, and a blank screen beckoning for attention.

Determined to demonstrate that he's still "with it," Povey boasts of his youthful vigour and insists on showing Lt. Havers a thing or two about strategy—conveniently using the 'arcade game' as a tool to exhibit his prowess. Unbeknownst to him, the machine is actually a missile system's computer, casually left in the mess by an overworked engineer amidst repair efforts.

Povey’s vigorous toggling and strategic button pressing is met with cheers and jokes from the crew, who gather around this unexpected source of amusement. Havers, trying to uphold a smidge of decorum, plays along only to realize, to his horror, that these "game moves" are being mirrored by real-life ballistic protocols.

Meanwhile, WREN Underwood notices an unusual number of dummy missiles being prepped on deck—supposedly for a routine exercise—and quickly recognizes the terrible mistake. As Povey executes a 'gaming' move that would supposedly defeat the enemy base, a series of dummy missiles launch spectacularly into the sea, causing shock and awe among the crew.

Povey, ever oblivious, basks in his triumphant 'high score', while WREN Underwood and Lt. Havers scramble to abort further commands and contain the situation. With a composed facade, Havers turns to Povey and says, “You see, sir, your unconventional tactics are truly... making waves. Although next time, perhaps a war room might be more appropriate than the mess hall?”

In the debriefing, Povey, mortified but not ready to face his folly, mutters something about "testing emergency response times" while the crew exchange knowing glances. CPO Pertwee quips, “If that was a videogame, sir, I reckon it’s back to the drawing board for level one!”

"The Accidental Arcade" presents a light-hearted jab at the emerging video game culture of the 1980s, blending the timeless navy humour with a touch of new-tech satire. The misunderstandings, mistaken identities, and enthusiastic display of non-existent gaming skills come together to create an episode of 'The Navy Lark' that would resonate with the culture and technology conversations of the time.

The Reveal: The drill concludes, and Captain Povey invites the dignitary to dine with him. As they're discussing seafaring stories, the dignitary lets slip a detail about a past misadventure that only an old shipmate could know—a comedic escapade involving Pertwee during their training days.

The crew is half-relieved and half-exasperated. Pertwee, in particular, is torn between outrage and amusement.

End of Episode: Classic Navy Lark laughter follows, and the episode's music plays out.

Episode Title: "A Substitution of Sorts"

Opening Scene: Captain Povey paces his office as he receives word that an honorary senior female officer's arrival will be postponed. A television crew from the BBC's programme on the 'Modern Navy' has already settled on HMS Troutbridge to document her integration into the crew. Povey frets over the navy's image and the importance of demonstrating gender inclusivity.

Act One: WREN Underwood proposes a bold plan to prevent the cancellation of the filming. Povey takes the bait, desperate to maintain the ship’s image. He task Lt. Havers with the responsibility of finding a stand-in until the real officer arrives. Pertwee, overhearing the conversation, is vehemently opposed to the farce, insisting it's bound to blow back on them all.

Act Two: Underwood enlists the help of the crew to transform one of their own into the dignified persona of the senior female officer—think wigs, uniforms, and a crash course in 'officer-like' decorum. The selected crew member (a young seaman or even Lt. Havers himself in an act of duty and desperation) struggles with the high heels and high expectations. Pertwee's reluctance only grows, fearing the cross-dressing comedy will lead to a crossfire of criticism.

Act Three: The stand-in’s comically awkward behaviour is just short of revealing the ruse to the eager film crew capturing her every word—until the real female officer arrives on deck much sooner than expected. She’s unexpectedly down-to-earth, creating an engaging contrast with her supposed impersonator, stirring up nervous energy among the crew.

The Climax: As the truth unfolds, the officer takes the revelation in stride, amused by the crew's well-intentioned efforts to keep up appearances. The film crew, initially dumbfounded, decides it makes for even more interesting television, showcasing the crew's dedication to modernization, albeit through unconventional means.

Closing Scene: With the day's filming wrapped, the crew collectively sighs in relief. CPO Pertwee, impressed by the real officer's handling of the situation, quips: 

**CPO Pertwee**: "Well, you have to give it to her—she took that better than a room full of Marines would’ve! And as for the rest of you lot, involved in this pantomime... if you ever suggest I wear anything but my uniform again, I’ll have you all swabbing the decks until you dream of buckets and mops!"

End of Episode: The camera crew packs away, the real officer becomes part of the team, and life on HMS Troutbridge returns to its typical, less theatrically-inclined routine. The Navy Lark’s signature music plays, signaling the end of another delightfully farcical voyage.
